figures.caption: Table 1. Ablation studies on three evaluation sets: Algorithmic (Algo.), Random (Rand.), and Production (Prod.). Left: Comparison of prediction head variants shows that our AIS layer consistently outperforms alternative prediction heads. Right: Comparison of training input schedules highlights the importance of aligning training and inference schedules. On the Prod. Set, the DBA schedule with second level of augmentation outperforms random schedules.

Table 2. Comparison of our model against SOTA baselines across both our In-House Dataset (Keyframe-Based) and the LaFAN1 Dataset [Harvey et al. 2020] (MOCAP). Our AIS-BiLSTM model outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on all metrics for the Keyframe-Based dataset, and achieves competitive results on LaFAN1, highlighting its effectiveness for production-style animation as well as general motion data.

Fig. 5. Qualitative results from the Algo. Test Set (left) and the Prod. Test Set (right); ground truth and input keyposes in color, predictions in grayscale. We compare ground truth (GT) and Maya’s native spline interpolation (Maya) against our method (AIS-BiLSTM). Spline interpolation fails to create breakdown poses, produces self-penetration artifacts, and yields overly smooth motion, while our method generates plausible breakdowns, reproduces effects such as Ease In, and replicates the intended snappy style with held poses.

Fig. 6. Qualitative results of our AIS-BiLSTM model trained on the LaFAN1 dataset (GT/inputs: color, predictions: grayscale). The model successfully learns smooth, human-plausible motion styles, with correct intermediate poses. However, as shown in the second example (red box), the model fails to predict an intermediate step in the running sequence. This indicates a limitation in inferring missing content over extended intervals.

Fig. 7. Illustration of sensitivity to the inference keypose schedule (GT/inputs: color, predictions: grayscale). "DBA Pred." infers from the DBA schedule used at training (two boundary keyposes), while "Prod. Pred." infers from a production schedule defined by animators, who added a middle keypose. The DBA prediction produces stable motion with held poses and a snappy transition, while the production prediction fails: the first pose is not held (red box) and the added middle keypose is over-held (orange box).

Fig. 2. Overview of our AIS-BiLSTM architecture. Left: The Domain-Based Algorithm (DBA) schedule selects training input keyposes to form the masked sequence 𝑆0, encoded by a Bi-LSTM into hidden states ℎ for the AIS layer. Middle: AIS layer zoom at frame 𝑡 . Hidden state ℎ𝑡 drives three MLPs predicting interpolation weights 𝛼𝑡 , synthesis pose 𝑝 synth
